name: CNH Industrial N.V.

profile.description: CNH Industrial N.V. develops, manufactures, and sells agricultural and construction equipment globally through three segments: Agriculture, Construction, and Financial Services. Brands include Case IH, New Holland, STEYR, Raven, Case Construction, and Eurocomach. Products span tractors, harvesters, excavators, loaders, and precision agriculture solutions. The Financial Services segment provides end-user and dealer financing. Founded in 1842, headquartered in Basildon, UK, with approximately 34,200 employees.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): CNH acquired Raven Industries in 2022 for $2.1B, significantly advancing its precision agriculture capabilities and autonomous technology platform. The company has integrated Raven's autonomy solutions into its Case IH and New Holland product lines, and launched a refreshed agricultural equipment lineup for 2026 [2][3]. The SenseApply sprayer automation system uses machine vision to reduce herbicide use by 80%, representing a meaningful product innovation. AI Deployment (2/2): CNH has deployed AI with measurable results across operations. The AI Tech Assistant launched in early 2025 serves hundreds of dealer groups for machinery diagnostics and repair plans [3]. AI-enabled spraying reduces herbicide use by 80%. AI assists software engineers on FieldOps, reducing documentation time by 60%. Every member of the global leadership team runs at least one generative or agentic AI pilot [3]. Market Share (1/2): CNH is one of two dominant agricultural equipment manufacturers globally alongside Deere & Company, with strong positions in North America, Europe, and South America. However, Deere maintains market share leadership in most categories [2]. Product Pipeline (1/2): CNH targets 90% in-house precision technology by 2030 (up from 80%) and plans to nearly double Precision Tech's share of Agriculture Net Sales by 2030 [3]. Specific product timelines beyond the 2026 lineup refresh are less defined. R&D Intensity (0/1): R&D spending has increased, but CNH underperforms Deere on absolute R&D investment as a percentage of agricultural revenue.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): CEO Garrit Marx has articulated a clear purpose: 'Agriculture's biggest challenge is to feed more people, with less land, under increasingly difficult conditions' [3]. This connects CNH's work to a meaningful global challenge (food security) and identifies the customer (farmers facing rising demands). The purpose is specific and thoughtful but has not yet been validated through decades of consistent execution under this framing — Marx took the helm in July 2024. Community Impact (1/2): CNH's 11 consecutive years on the Dow Jones Sustainability Indices (DJSI) World and Europe, with the highest score (88/100) in Machinery and Electrical Equipment, demonstrates sustained commitment to stakeholder value [4]. Specific programs include human rights, human capital development, and supply chain management scoring highest in its peer group. Employee Retention (1/2): CNH's 34,197 employees work within a culture described as focused on innovation, engineering excellence, and global collaboration [3]. The new Global Leadership Team structure emphasizes empowering domain experts. However, the company underwent significant leadership changes in 2024 (new CEO, restructured leadership team), which creates short-term uncertainty. Insider Buying (1/2): CEO Marx assumed direct responsibility for the Agriculture segment, signaling deep personal commitment, but no specific insider buying with dollar amounts was identified.

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on Growth sleeve metrics. Q1 2026 revenue of $3.83B was essentially flat YoY, but net income plunged to $10M from $132M due to tariff impacts, lower North America/South America volumes, and higher SG&A/R&D expenses [1]. Full-year 2025 revenue was $18.1B, down from $19.8B in 2024 and $24.7B in 2023 — a steep cyclical decline. D/E of 333.7% scores 0.0 (floor exceeded, driven by Financial Services subsidiary leverage). ROE of 4.8% scores 0.32 at 2x weight. Revenue growth YoY of -0.1% scores 0.66. 3-year revenue CAGR proxy of -8.4% scores 0.0 at 2x weight. Forward P/E of 14.1 scores 1.0. Profit margin of 2.1% scores 0.11. PEG ratio of 0.58 scores 1.0 at 2x weight. Weighted sum 4.42 / max 10 = 4.4, rounds to 4. 2026 outlook: industrial net sales flat to down 4%, adjusted EPS $0.35-$0.45, with 210-220bps tariff drag on ag margins [1].

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): CNH's core business — manufacturing heavy agricultural and construction equipment — is structurally immune to AI disruption. Physical tractors, harvesters, excavators, and loaders cannot be replaced by software. The manufacturing process involves complex mechanical engineering, global supply chains, steel fabrication, and dealer service networks that AI cannot substitute [1][3]. AI is primarily an enabler for CNH: precision agriculture increases equipment value, autonomous technology creates differentiation, and AI diagnostics improve dealer service efficiency. If anything, AI increases demand for smart agricultural equipment. The $26B+ Financial Services portfolio (equipment financing) also benefits from AI-enhanced credit decisions. AI Opportunity (1/1): CNH generates measurable AI-driven value. The AI Tech Assistant serves hundreds of dealer groups [3]. SenseApply sprayer automation uses machine learning to cut herbicide use by 80%. AI reduces engineering documentation time by 60%. The 2030 strategy to nearly double Precision Tech's share of Agriculture Net Sales represents significant AI-enabled revenue growth potential [3].

infinite_game.summary: CNH Industrial traces its heritage to 1842, demonstrating institutional endurance through 184 years of agricultural cycles, wars, and economic crises. CEO Marx's articulation of feeding the world as the core challenge reflects genuine purpose. The company's 11 consecutive years on DJSI and substantial investment in precision agriculture technology signal long-term thinking. However, the 2024 CEO transition, steep earnings decline (net income from $132M to $10M in Q1), volatile dividend policy, and heavy Financial Services leverage create finite-game pressures around quarterly performance management.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): CEO Marx's framing — 'Agriculture's biggest challenge is to feed more people, with less land, under increasingly difficult conditions' — articulates a genuine cause that connects CNH's work to global food security [3]. This cause is inclusive, service-oriented, and bigger than CNH itself. The company's 184-year heritage validates long-term commitment to agriculture. However, Marx only assumed the CEO role in July 2024, and the cause has not yet been tested through the type of costly strategic decisions Sinek values. The heavy emphasis on precision tech revenue targets suggests the cause may be more strategic narrative than lived purpose.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): CNH's restructured Global Leadership Team empowers domain experts with 'intimate in-house and industry knowledge' to execute strategic priorities [3]. The company scored highest in its DJSI peer group on Human Capital Development, suggesting investment in employee development [4]. However, the July 2024 CEO change and Global Leadership Team restructuring created organizational disruption. Q1 2026 SG&A expenses increased, and the steep earnings decline creates pressure that can erode psychological safety in the short term.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): CNH consistently uses Deere & Company's strengths as motivation to improve rather than adopting a zero-sum competitive mindset. The 2030 precision technology strategy (90% in-house, double Precision Tech revenue share) is framed around capability building rather than 'beating Deere' [3]. CNH's AI Tech Assistant and SenseApply innovations respond to competitive pressure by building differentiated capabilities, not by attacking competitors.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): CNH has evolved meaningfully from a traditional mechanical equipment manufacturer to a technology-enabled precision agriculture company. The $2.1B Raven Industries acquisition (2022) was a genuine strategic pivot toward autonomous farming technology [3]. The shift from selling equipment to selling precision agriculture solutions and data-driven farming represents real business model evolution. However, the core remains manufacturing physical machinery — the pivot is additive rather than transformative in Sinek's canonical sense.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): CNH invested $2.1B to acquire Raven Industries during a period of strong cyclical earnings, betting on autonomous farming technology for the long term rather than returning all cash to shareholders [3]. Increasing R&D spending during the current cyclical downturn (Q1 2026 R&D expenses rose YoY) while earnings collapsed 92% demonstrates willingness to invest through the cycle [1]. However, the 2026 guidance (flat to -4% revenue, $0.35-$0.45 EPS) suggests management is managing expectations rather than making bold contrarian bets.

investment_thesis: CNH Industrial is a 184-year-old agricultural and construction equipment manufacturer trading at an attractive 14.1x forward P/E with a PEG ratio of 0.58, reflecting the current agricultural downturn cycle [1][5]. CEO Marx has designated agriculture as his primary focus and is investing in AI-powered precision technology (SenseApply sprayer cutting herbicide 80%, AI Tech Assistant for dealers, 90% in-house precision tech target by 2030) that should drive higher-margin revenue as the cycle recovers [3]. The company's DJSI leadership (88/100 score, 11 consecutive years) and Inspire Alignment score of 64 reflect strong ESG positioning. Q1 2026 revenue of $3.83B was flat YoY, suggesting the bottom may be forming after two years of revenue decline from the $24.7B peak in 2023.
thesis_continued: The bear case is cyclical severity and structural leverage. Net income collapsed 92% YoY to $10M in Q1 2026, with profit margin at just 2.1% and ROE at 4.8% [1]. D/E of 333.7% (driven by $26.3B Financial Services debt) is the highest in the equipment industry and creates refinancing risk if agricultural credit quality deteriorates. Revenue has fallen 27% from the $24.7B 2023 peak to the $15.3B annualized Q1 2026 run-rate. Tariffs create 210-220bps drag on agricultural margins and 600bps on construction margins in 2026 [1]. The dividend yield of 2.4% is unreliable — 5yr dividend growth CAGR is -5.4%, and the $0.25 annual dividend represents a 78% payout ratio that is unsustainable if earnings continue to compress. Global agricultural equipment demand is expected to decline a further 5% in 2026, and CNH's revenue cycle may not trough until 2027.
key_catalysts: Agricultural cycle recovery beginning late 2026 or 2027 would drive significant earnings leverage given CNH's operating structure — revenue returning toward $20B+ would dramatically improve margins from the current 2.1% trough

Precision agriculture revenue doubling as a share of Agriculture Net Sales by 2030, driven by SenseApply, autonomous technology from Raven, and AI-powered dealer tools, would structurally improve margins

PEG ratio of 0.58 implies significant earnings recovery is priced in — if management achieves the midpoint of $0.35-$0.45 EPS guidance and cycle recovery begins, multiple expansion is possible from the depressed 14x forward P/E
key_risks: D/E of 333.7% from $26.3B Financial Services debt creates refinancing and credit quality risk — agricultural loan delinquencies could spike if the downturn extends and farmer incomes remain depressed
Tariff impacts of 210-220bps on agricultural margins and 600bps on construction margins in 2026 represent a new structural headwind that compresses already-thin 2.1% profit margins

Global agricultural equipment demand declining a further 5% in 2026 means the cyclical trough may not arrive until 2027, with risk of additional dividend cuts from the current unsustainable 78% payout ratio
